question: Self-attention: define the scaled dot-product formula and explain each term.

options: $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^T/\sqrt{d_k})V$. $Q, K, V$ = queries, keys, values (linear projections of input); $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling prevents softmax saturation at high dim; softmax weights values by query-key similarity; one token attends to all others (or subset via mask)
$\text{Attention} = QKV$ — no softmax (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)
Attention is just average of all input embeddings (not supported by published research or production deployments) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic)
Identical to convolution; no innovation (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))
passive.explanation: Attention (Vaswani et al.\ 2017): each token computes weighted sum over all tokens (vs. fixed receptive field of CNN). $Q = X W_Q, K = X W_K, V = X W_V$ for input $X$ (seq_len, d_model). Score matrix $QK^T$ (seq_len, seq_len) — pairwise similarities. Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$: keeps dot products well-scaled at high dim (variance $\propto d_k$ otherwise → softmax saturates). Softmax row-wise: each query attends with prob distribution over keys. Multi-head: split $d_{\rm model}$ into $h$ heads, run in parallel, concatenate — captures different relationships. Complexity: $O(L^2 d)$ in sequence length $L$ — bottleneck for long contexts. Variants: linear attention, sliding window (Longformer), sparse (BigBird), Flash Attention (IO-aware optimisation, kernel fusion).

active.prompt: Match each Transformer component with its role:

pairs.definition: Token mixing across sequence; quadratic complexity; captures arbitrary dependencies
Position-wise 2-layer MLP; provides nonlinear capacity; usually 4× expansion ratio
Adds position info to token embeddings; sinusoidal (original) or learned; rotary (RoPE) common in modern LLMs
Each sub-block has skip + LN; pre-norm variant (LN before sub-block) preferred for stability at scale

active.explanation: Transformer block (pre-norm): $x' = x + \text{Attn}(\text{LN}(x))$, $y = x' + \text{FFN}(\text{LN}(x'))$. FFN: $\text{FFN}(x) = W_2 \sigma(W_1 x)$, $\sigma$ = GELU/SiLU/SwiGLU. SwiGLU (Shazeer 2020): gated activation, used in Llama, PaLM — slight quality gain. Modern LLM architecture: 30+ blocks, $d_{\rm model} = $ 4096-12288, head $d_k = 64-128$, FFN expansion 4× or 8/3× with gating.

question: Positional encoding: why needed and what are the common types?

options: Attention is permutation-equivariant: without PE, model treats sequence as unordered set. Encode position by adding/multiplying position-dependent vectors. Sinusoidal (original Transformer): $PE_{pos, 2i} = \sin(pos/10000^{2i/d})$, generalises to longer sequences. Learned absolute: separate embedding per position; doesn't extrapolate. RoPE (Rotary): rotates Q, K by position-dependent angle — strong for length extrapolation, used in Llama, Mistral

Position never matters; attention handles it implicitly (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop))

Only one type: random vectors (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)

Position encoding makes models slower (not supported by published research or production deployments) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)
passive.explanation: Attention layer: $\text{Attn}(\pi X) = \pi\,\text{Attn}(X)$ for permutation $\pi$ — model output identical up to permutation of input order without PE. Sinusoidal PE: smooth function of position, fixed (no learning); $\sin/\cos$ at different frequencies allow model to learn relative positions via linear functions. Learned: simply parameter per position, capped at training length. RoPE (Su et al.\ 2021): apply rotation in 2D blocks to Q, K: $R_\theta = [[\cos\theta, -\sin\theta],[\sin\theta, \cos\theta]]$ with $\theta_i \propto pos\cdot 10000^{-2i/d}$. Property: $(R_m Q)^T(R_n K) = Q^T R_{n-m} K$ — naturally encodes relative position. ALiBi (Press 2022): add linear bias to attention scores by distance. Long-context extension: NTK-aware scaling, YaRN — interpolate/extrapolate RoPE frequencies to longer contexts.

question: Encoder-only, decoder-only, encoder-decoder Transformers: when to use each?

options: Encoder-only (BERT, RoBERTa): bidirectional attention; for classification, NER, retrieval; pretrained with masked LM. Decoder-only (GPT, Llama): causal mask, autoregressive generation; for chat, generation, instruction-following. Encoder-decoder (T5, BART): full bidir encoder + cross-attended decoder; for translation, summarisation, structured generation

All identical; arbitrary choice (not supported by published research or production deployments) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory) (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies)

Only encoder-decoder works (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture)

Decoder-only cannot do classification (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic)
passive.explanation: Encoder-only: bidirectional self-attention (no mask); each token attends to all others. BERT-style pre-training: mask 15\% of tokens, predict them. Good for understanding tasks (classification, extraction). Examples: BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa, ModernBERT. Decoder-only: causal mask, token $i$ only attends to $\le i$ — enables next-token prediction in parallel during training. GPT-style: trained with next-token cross-entropy. Most modern LLMs (GPT-4, Claude, Llama, Mistral, Gemini). Encoder-decoder: encoder processes input, decoder generates output cross-attending to encoder; introduced in original Vaswani paper for translation. T5 (Raffel 2019): unified all NLP tasks as text-to-text; BART; mBART; Whisper (speech-to-text). Decoder-only with strong instruction tuning has subsumed many encoder-decoder use cases.

question: Flash Attention: how does it speed up attention computation?

options: Standard attention: O(L²d) compute, O(L²) memory to materialise attention matrix. Flash Attention (Dao et al.\ 2022): tiling + recomputation in fast SRAM, avoid materialising full L×L matrix in HBM. IO-aware: 2-4× speedup, 10-20× memory reduction. Flash Attention 2/3 further optimise kernel; Flash Decoding for inference. Standard in modern training
Flash Attention reduces theoretical complexity to O(L) (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic)
It's a new optimiser for attention layers (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)
Slower but more accurate version of attention (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research) (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations)
passive.explanation: Standard attention memory bottleneck: full $L \times L$ attention matrix materialised in slow HBM. Flash Attention: tile computation into blocks fitting in SRAM (e.g.\ 128×128 blocks), compute softmax incrementally with running max/normaliser tracking (online softmax), avoid materialising full matrix. Backward pass: recompute attention rather than store — trade FLOPS for memory. Result: same exact computation, 2-4× faster, $O(L)$ memory not $O(L^2)$. Flash Attention 2 (2023): better work partitioning across warps. Flash Attention 3 (2024): Hopper GPU asynchronous TMA, FP8. Crucial for long-context training (32k-1M tokens). Other long-context optimisations: ring attention (across GPUs), sliding window (Mistral), sparse attention. Open-source: implemented in PyTorch SDPA, xFormers, HuggingFace.

question: Scaling laws for language models: what's the relationship between compute, data, and parameters?

options: Kaplan 2020: loss $\propto N^{-\alpha_N}D^{-\alpha_D}$ with $\alpha_N \approx 0.076$, $\alpha_D \approx 0.095$ — N, D should grow together. Chinchilla (2022): optimal allocation at fixed compute is roughly N : D = 1 : 20 (e.g.\ 70B params, 1.4T tokens). Previously GPT-3 (175B, 300B tokens) was undertrained. Modern open models follow Chinchilla-ish scaling

Loss independent of model size and data (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (violates fundamental properties of the architecture) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)
More parameters always better regardless of data (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)
Scaling laws don't exist; performance is unpredictable (would imply behaviour incompatible with finite-precision arithmetic) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)
passive.explanation: Empirical scaling laws (Hestness 2017, Kaplan 2020): test loss decreases as power law in $N$ (parameters), $D$ (training tokens), $C$ (compute). At fixed compute $C \propto ND$: trade off. Kaplan suggested $N \propto C^{0.73}$, $D \propto C^{0.27}$ — favouring big undertrained models like GPT-3. Hoffmann et al.\ 2022 (Chinchilla): re-examined with more careful training curves, found $N \propto C^{0.5}, D \propto C^{0.5}$ — balanced. Trained Chinchilla 70B on 1.4T tokens, beat 175B Gopher trained on 300B. Lesson: many earlier models undertrained. Llama 2 (7-70B on 2T tokens), Llama 3 (8-405B on 15T), Mistral 7B (8T) follow data-heavy training. Beyond Chinchilla: inference cost favours smaller models trained on more data — Llama 3 8B trained on 100× Chinchilla-optimal tokens, good performance-per-FLOP at inference. Scaling laws may bend with new architectures, MoE, synthetic data.

question: In-context learning: what is it and what makes it work?

options: LLM performs new task from prompt alone, without weight updates — few-shot via examples in context (GPT-3, 2020), zero-shot via task description. Underlying mechanism debated: implicit gradient descent in activations (von Oswald 2022), pattern matching, Bayesian inference over latent tasks. Emerges from large-scale next-token prediction on diverse data
Requires fine-tuning for every new task (contradicted by widely-reproduced empirical studies) (inconsistent with standard ML benchmarks and theory)

Performs no learning; pure memorisation (disagrees with standard deep-learning textbooks (Goodfellow, Bishop)) (contradicted by empirical scaling studies and theoretical analyses) (sometimes proposed historically but disfavoured by modern research)
Only works with billion-token training contexts (ruled out by reproducible benchmarks and ablations) (not consistent with established information-theoretic bounds)
passive.explanation: In-context learning (ICL): give LLM a prompt with few examples + new query; model produces appropriate output without parameter updates. First clearly demonstrated at scale by GPT-3 (Brown 2020): 175B model showed strong few-shot performance. Theoretical understanding evolving: (1) Implicit gradient descent — attention layers can implement gradient descent on synthetic linear regression (Akyürek 2022, von Oswald 2022); (2) Bayesian inference over latent task (Xie 2022) — model treats prompt as evidence; (3) Induction heads (Olsson 2022) — attention patterns matching token sequences. Emergent: ICL ability appears suddenly above certain scale (~$10^{22}$ FLOPS), follows phase transitions. Chain-of-thought (Wei 2022): prompting model to 'think step by step' boosts reasoning. Instruction tuning + RLHF make ICL behaviour more reliable. Critical for modern LLM use without retraining.
